eli3: An ML pipeline is like a factory assembly line: raw data comes in one end, gets processed through multiple stages (cleaning, feature engineering, training, evaluation), and a trained model comes out the other end.

The pipeline has distinct phases: data collection, data validation, transformation, model training, model validation, and deployment. Each stage feeds into the next, with feedback loops for monitoring and retraining when performance degrades.

Unlike traditional software, ML pipelines are probabilistic and data-dependent. The same code can produce different models with different data, making versioning, reproducibility, and testing critical challenges.

whenWhy.title: Production ML systems
whenWhy.body: Any ML model serving real users needs a pipeline. Companies like Netflix, Uber, and Google run thousands of pipelines daily for recommendations, pricing, and search. Pipelines enable automation, scalability, and reliability.

whenWhy.title: Reproducibility and experimentation
whenWhy.body: Pipelines version data, code, and models together. Data scientists can reproduce experiments, compare model versions, and understand what changed. Essential for regulated industries (finance, healthcare) and debugging.

whenWhy.title: Continuous training and deployment
whenWhy.body: Pipelines enable automated retraining when new data arrives or performance degrades. CI/CD for ML automates testing, validation, and safe rollout. Reduces time from experiment to production from months to days.

whenWhy.title: Collaboration across teams
whenWhy.body: Data engineers own ingestion, ML engineers own training, DevOps owns deployment. Clear pipeline stages define interfaces and responsibilities. Feature stores and model registries act as shared contracts between teams.

deepDiveIntro: A production ML pipeline typically includes: (1) Data ingestion and validation — ensuring quality and schema consistency; (2) Feature engineering — transforming raw data into model inputs; (3) Training — fitting models on prepared data; (4) Evaluation — validating on held-out data; (5) Model validation — ensuring production readiness; (6) Deployment — serving predictions; (7) Monitoring — tracking performance and data drift; (8) Retraining — updating models with new data.

Pipeline orchestration tools (Airflow, Kubeflow, Metaflow) manage dependencies, scheduling, and retries. Data versioning (DVC) tracks datasets. Model registries (MLflow) version artifacts. Together they enable reproducible, scalable ML systems.

deepDive.heading: Pipeline Components
deepDive.body: Data ingestion: batch (Spark, BigQuery) or streaming (Kafka, Flink). Validation: Great Expectations, TensorFlow Data Validation (TFDV). Feature engineering: batch (pandas, Spark) or online (Flink, Redis). Training: distributed (Horovod, Ray Train) or single-node. Evaluation: holdout sets, cross-validation, A/B tests. Deployment: batch (scheduled jobs), real-time (APIs), or embedded (mobile/edge).
deepDive.formula: Pipeline: Data → Validation → Features → Train → Evaluate → Validate → Deploy → Monitor → Retrain

deepDive.heading: Data Validation and Schema
deepDive.body: Schema defines expected data types, ranges, and distributions. Validation catches training-serving skew, data drift, and anomalies. TFDV computes statistics and infers schemas. Great Expectations defines expectations (e.g., 'column X should have mean between 0 and 1'). Invalid data triggers alerts or pipeline failures.

deepDive.heading: Training-Serving Skew
deepDive.body: Discrepancy between data processing at training and inference time. Causes: (1) different code paths, (2) different data sources, (3) temporal leakage. Solution: shared transformation code, feature stores for consistent online/offline features, and careful test set design to prevent leakage.
deepDive.formula: Skew = E[loss(inference)] - E[loss(training)] due to distribution mismatch

deepDive.heading: Pipeline Orchestration
deepDive.body: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of tasks with dependencies. Apache Airflow: mature, Python-based, good for batch. Kubeflow Pipelines: Kubernetes-native, integrates with ML workflows. Metaflow: Netflix-born, handles complexity elegantly. Prefect: modern Airflow alternative. Key features: retry logic, parallel execution, resource management, observability.
deepDive.formula: DAG G = (V, E) where V = tasks, E = dependencies

deepDive.heading: Reproducibility
deepDive.body: Deterministic pipelines: version data (DVC), code (git), dependencies (Docker, requirements.txt), and random seeds. Containerization (Docker) ensures consistent environments. Experiment tracking (MLflow, Weights & Biases) logs hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts. Reproducibility enables debugging, auditing, and collaboration.

takeaways: ML pipeline = sequence of stages from data to deployed model.
Key stages: ingestion, validation, features, training, evaluation, deployment, monitoring, retraining.
Training-serving skew is a major source of production failures.
Orchestration tools manage DAGs, retries, and resource allocation.
Reproducibility requires versioning data, code, dependencies, and environments.

edgeCases.q: What happens when data validation fails in production?
edgeCases.a: Options: (1) Stop pipeline and alert — safest but interrupts service; (2) Skip validation — dangerous, may serve bad predictions; (3) Fallback to previous model — safer but may be stale; (4) Use default/imputed values — depends on use case. Best practice: fail fast in training, have degradation strategies for inference.

edgeCases.q: How do you handle temporal data in pipelines?
edgeCases.a: Critical for time-series and causal inference. Never use future data to predict past (data leakage). Use sliding windows for training. Point-in-time correctness: features must be computed as of prediction time, not data arrival time. Feature stores handle this by versioning features by timestamp.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between batch and streaming pipelines?
edgeCases.a: Batch: process data in chunks on schedule (hourly, daily). Higher latency, easier to debug, simpler failure recovery. Streaming: process events as they arrive (milliseconds latency). More complex (windowing, watermarking, state management), harder to debug, requires stream processing frameworks (Flink, Spark Streaming). Hybrid: Lambda or Kappa architectures.

sources.label: Huyen — Designing Machine Learning Systems, Ch. 3-4
Google — ML Test Score: Rules for ML Engineering
